questions.question: According to boundary theory, what predicts wellbeing better than the objective separation between work and personal life?

options.text: The number of hours you work per week
Boundary control — your ability to DECIDE when work enters personal time
Whether you work from home or in an office
How much you enjoy your job

questions.explanation: Research shows that boundary control — your perceived ability to decide when work ends and personal life begins — is more important than the objective amount of separation. High control + high permeability (work-life integration by choice) can be protective, while low control + high permeability (work-life invasion) is harmful. Control is the key variable.

questions.question: What is 'psychological detachment' and why does it matter?

options.text: Psychological detachment means not caring about your work
Psychological detachment is the ability to mentally disengage from work during non-work time, and it predicts recovery, sleep quality, and reduced exhaustion
Psychological detachment is a mental health disorder
Psychological detachment only matters for people with anxiety

questions.explanation: Psychological detachment is the ability to mentally disengage from work during off-hours — to stop thinking about work problems, tasks, or stressors. Research shows it's a robust predictor of recovery, sleep quality, and reduced exhaustion. It's teachable through transition rituals, hobbies, physical separation, and boundary-setting practices.

questions.question: When is 'flexible work' protective for mental health, and when is it harmful?

options.text: Flexible work is protective when it's voluntary and employee-controlled; it's harmful when it's involuntary and employer-controlled (unpredictable schedules)
Flexible work only matters for parents

questions.explanation: Research shows that voluntary, employee-controlled flexibility is associated with reduced stress and higher job satisfaction. However, involuntary or unpredictable flexibility — where work hours are variable and controlled by the employer, not the employee — is associated with worse mental health outcomes including exhaustion and emotional distress. Employee control is the key.

questions.question: Which of the following boundary challenges is STRUCTURAL (organizational) rather than individual (skills-based)?

options.text: You have permission to disconnect but struggle with anxiety about missing messages
Your organization punishes employees who don't respond to emails within minutes, even on weekends
You need scripts for communicating boundaries assertively
Expectations are unclear, so you default to over-availability

questions.explanation: If your organization's culture punishes boundaries or expects constant availability (and penalizes those who don't comply), that's a structural Psychosocial Safety Climate problem. Individual boundary-setting skills won't solve this — it requires organizational policy change or, if the organization won't change, exit. The other options are individual skills challenges.
